figures.caption: Table 1: Architectural Comparison of Automation-Exploit against State-of-the-Art Paradigms.

Figure 3: Autopsy-driven Feedback and Self-Healing Mechanism. When an unstable payload triggers a critical failure within the Digital Twin, the Autopsy System intercepts the crash telemetry. This deterministic feedback is processed to autonomously deploy a fresh replica and provide register-level intelligence to the generative agents for the subsequent iteration.
Figure 6: Total Operational Time Distribution. Phase 1 (Reconnaissance) accounts for 77.2% of the execution time, while the Digital Twin (Isomorphic Sync) requires only 2.9%.

Figure 2: Digital Twin Safety Layer and Unified Forensic Engine. Container A maintains isomorphic fidelity with the actual target via state synchronization, while Container B safely executes a 7-stage forensic pipeline to extract vulnerability blueprints without risking DoS on the replica.

Figure 5: Cognitive Adaptation Dynamics. Action Executability Rate (AER) versus False Positive Rate (FPR) during Stage 1 across all eight scenarios. AER remains above 85% in five scenarios; FPR peaks are statistical artifacts of low iteration counts and are fully neutralised by Stage 2 Adversarial Auditing.
Figure 1: Cognitive Self-Healing Loop. Flow of the “Adversarial Hand-off” between local and cloud models, mediated by the Orchestrator and TAG.

Table 5: Summary of experimental KPIs across all eight evaluation scenarios. GER: Global Efficiency Ratio; TTC: Time-to-Compromise; FPR: False Positive Rate (generative hallucinations); AER: Action Executability Rate.

Figure 4: Efficiency-Time Correlation. Global Efficiency Ratio (GER) versus Time-to-Compromise (TTC) across all eight scenarios. High GER is maintained independently of TTC; drops in Scenarios F and G reflect constrained binary surfaces requiring exhaustive exploration.
Table 3: Taxonomy of experimental scenarios and validated architectural components.
